title: Rules of Craft
description: Rules of the Craft examines the conditions that shape the act of making. Rather than focusing on final outcomes, this section looks at process—how decisions are formed, interrupted, and redirected over time. Each rule operates within the moment of creation, influencing how tools are used, how compositions develop, and how ideas are carried forward or abandoned. Some rules impose clear limitations: restricting materials, removing the ability to edit, or forcing continuous progression without revision. Others are more subtle, introducing hesitation, repetition, or shifts in attention. Together, they create a framework where control is never absolute and where the process itself becomes unstable, responsive, and open to change. These constraints do not produce a single method or aesthetic. Instead, they generate variation. The same rule can lead to entirely different approaches depending on how it is interpreted, resisted, or misunderstood in practice. Process becomes less about efficiency or refinement, and more about navigation—working within, against, and around the conditions in place. What emerges is a record of making that is layered, inconsistent, and often unresolved. Iterations build on top of one another, decisions accumulate, and traces of earlier stages remain visible. The work carries evidence of its own construction, revealing not just what was made, but how it came into being. In this way, craft is not treated as a neutral skillset or a linear progression toward a finished piece. It is an active site of negotiation, where rules shape behavior, disrupt habits, and redefine what it means to complete something.

rules.author: Molly Vanderbogart
rules.description: A study in controlled unpredictability—using material limitations to generate motion and depth.
rules.process:
I started by creating a short base animation. I printed each frame on vellum, keeping every sheet aligned in the tray as the next one printed. This caused the ink to bleed and drag across the surface—something I had encountered before, but this time chose to use intentionally.

I then stacked the frames and scanned them together. The layering created a “ghosting” effect, where each frame subtly revealed the next, enhancing the sense of motion.

From there, I brought the scans into After Effects to reconstruct the animation, then moved into Photoshop to further manipulate the frames. After refining them, I returned to After Effects to build out the final animation.

Finally, I created an image by blending all the frames into a single static composition, compressing the motion into one moment.

rules.rule: Try to recreate your creative vision as best as you can.
rules.author: Hayley Rocker
rules.description: An exploration of how ideas evolve through iteration, distortion, and reinterpretation.
rules.process:
I set out to visualize how a creative vision shifts over time. Attempting to “recreate” an idea often involves constant iteration and unexpected change.

I intentionally degraded the composition across multiple iterations, allowing it to break down and transform. With each pass, the piece moved further from its original form while still retaining a sense of cohesion.

What began as a controlled idea, evolved into something entirely new—highlighting how the process itself can shape the final outcome.

rules.rule: Look for inspiration before you start a project
rules.author: Elysia Shannon

rules.rule: Design one step at a time, don't mix tasks.
rules.author: Kristen Sauer

rules.rule: Don't play it safe and experiment with new techniques
rules.author: Jubilee Harman
rules.description: A process-driven exploration combining unfamiliar materials and methods.
rules.process:
For this piece, I embraced experimentation by randomly generating three techniques: foiling, oil pastel, and collage. I began with a digital collage, converting it to black and white to prepare for toner-reactive foiling.

I printed the design onto nearly 20 sheets of black cardstock, then applied foil using heat. The foil adhered to the laser-printed areas, creating a reflective, high-contrast surface.

Next, I introduced oil pastel, applying it sporadically across each frame. I then physically scored another animation into the surface, carving into the layered materials to add another level of texture and disruption.

After scanning each frame back in, I reassembled them into an animation. The final result is highly textural and immersive—the text becomes partially illegible, but the piece shifts into something that is more about sensation than readability.

rules.rule: Map everything out first. Only focus on the fine-tuned layout last.
rules.author: Kiaran Lynch
rules.description: Breaking down composition into modular parts before resolving the final form.
rules.process:
I approached this piece by separating the design into individual components rather than focusing on a finalized layout from the start.

Each element was developed independently, allowing for flexibility and variation. I then combined these components into 13 distinct compositions, treating them like pieces of a puzzle that could be rearranged and reinterpreted.

rules.rule: Make small things, smaller pieces and projects can inspire and change bigger ones without all the pressure.
rules.author: Bryce Walsh
